question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?

SOURCE 50:
 you mention Conditional Access: as part of the migration baseline a CA policy was enabled requiring MFA for the "KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid" group. Could that be the issue?

31.01.25 16:34 Ticket closed | TI-1700004
Closure description:
Root cause was twofold. First, the service accounts were placed in the "KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid" group, which is the group targeted by the new tenant-baseline Conditional Access policy "KF70-Require-MFA". The Conditional Access evaluation was therefore being applied to non-interactive logons for service accounts, breaking scheduled-task authentication where MFA cannot be satisfied. Second, the service accounts had been moved to the new OU which is filtered IN by Azure AD Connect, leading to the accounts being synchronized to Entra ID and then having a license assigned (small but non-zero impact on the on-prem authentication path because of mfaPreferred attribute conflicts).
Resolution: Created a sub-group "KF70-ServiceAccounts-NoMFA" containing the service accounts and added it to the Conditional Access policy as an explicit exclusion. Re-evaluated AAD Connect filter to keep the on-prem service-account authentication path clean. Validated that scheduled tasks are now running successfully overnight.
Documentation note: For future migrations, service accounts must be explicitly excluded from baseline CA policies via a dedicated exclusion group, and their AAD Connect sync status should be reviewed to avoid unintended cloud-side license assignment. We have added this to the migration runbook.

Notes:

24.01.25 10:00 Employee 30
Initial diagnosis: scheduled tasks fail with logon-type denied. Customer confirms recent migration changed service-account group membership. Strong suspicion the new tenant-baseline Conditional Access policy is being evaluated against non-interactive service-account logons.
27.01.25 14:30 Employee 30
Confirmed via Azure AD sign-in logs: failed sign-in attempts for the service accounts are tagged "Failed - Conditional Access requires MFA". The CA policy targets the All-Users-Hybrid group which includes the service accounts.
28.01.25 11:00 Employee 30
Created KF70-ServiceAccounts-NoMFA exclusion group. Added service accounts. Updated CA policy to exclude this group. Sign-in logs now show successful authentication.
30.01.25 09:30 Employee 30
Customer confirms scheduled tasks ran successfully overnight without errors. Asked them to monitor for one more cycle before closure.
31.01.25 16:34 Microsoft CRM Portals
Ticket closed. Migration runbook updated with service-account handling guidance.

REPORT 209:
# Entra ID, Azure AD Connect, COMPLIANCE_TEAM and Hybrid Device Sync — Identity & Access Community

This community centers on the customer's Entra ID (Azure AD) tenant as the authoritative identity and access control plane for cloud sign‑in, group evaluation, Conditional Access, and device compliance state. Azure AD Connect synchronization (adjusted during a second migration wave to include the kf70group.local suffix) provisioned on‑prem objects into Entra ID and replaced an existing cloud‑native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with a synchronized on‑prem group that had a different Object ID. That Object‑ID replacement, combined with group nesting/evaluation behavior in Entra ID and SharePoint Online, produced an access mismatch that excluded external guest accounts. Separately, hybrid Azure AD–joined devices experienced delayed device synchronization (6–8 hours), causing device compliance to be stale/unknown and Conditional Access to evaluate sessions as non‑compliant until manual or subsequent sync completed; at least one affected user (Employee 21) manually triggered a device sync to expedite re‑evaluation. System Engineers used Microsoft Graph to enumerate and compare group memberships to support the analysis. These interactions identify operational dependencies (Entra ID, Azure AD Connect, SharePoint Online, hybrid device sync), recurring failure modes (group/object‑ID overwrite and device sync latency), and clear stakeholder touchpoints (synchronization operators, system engineers, affected users). [Data: Entities (366, 540, 1355, 1357, 544); Relationships (673, 1007, 2731, 2712, 1015)]

## Entra ID is the authoritative identity plane and the locus of the access-evaluation behavior

Entra ID (Azure AD) is documented as the tenant that manages user and group identities, records device compliance state, issues authentication tokens used by cloud services (including SharePoint Online), and produces sign‑in logs with Conditional Access evaluation results. This places Entra ID at the center of both authentication (token issuance, cloud sign‑in) and authorization (group/object‑ID evaluation used by SharePoint Online) for the affected users.
[Data: Entities (366)]

Because Entra ID evaluates groups by Object ID and records device compliance status (with several devices shown as "unknown"), misalignment in group Object IDs or stale device compliance states directly impact effective permissions and Conditional Access decisions. The report explicitly notes that a misconfigured or incorrect group nesting, combined with Object‑ID based evaluation and duplicate/unequal groups across sources, was identified as the root cause of the access mismatch—establishing an operational dependency where Entra ID’s evaluation logic determines access outcomes in SharePoint Online and Conditional Access. This makes Entra ID the control point for corrective actions such as reconciling object IDs, fixing group nests, or remediating device compliance visibility.
[Data: Entities (366)]

## Azure AD Connect synchronization during the second migration wave overwrote a cloud-native group with a synchronized on‑prem object (Object ID change)

Azure AD Connect was updated during the second migration wave to include the kf70group.local suffix, which made on‑prem identities eligible for synchronization into Entra ID; as a result Azure AD Connect wrote synchronized on‑prem objects into Entra ID and caused a pre‑existing cloud‑native group with the same name to be overwritten by the synchronized on‑prem group. The overwrite produced a different Object ID in Entra ID for the group, creating a divergence between the previously referenced cloud group and the newly synchronized object.
[Data: Relationships (673); Entities (366, 540)]

That Object‑ID replacement is the core technical mechanism that caused access mismatches: services and ACLs that reference the original cloud group by Object ID (or depend on the previous membership) observed a different membership set after synchronization. Because Entra ID evaluates effective permissions by Object ID, replacing or swapping objects with identical names but different Object IDs leads to authorization changes even when display names appear unchanged—an important operational risk when enabling large‑scale synchronization or changing AD Connect filters. This traces the causal chain from a sync/filter change to an authorization impact in cloud services.
[Data: Relationships (673); Entities (366)]

## COMPLIANCE_TEAM cloud-native group originally contained external guests and those guests lost effective access when the group was overwritten

A cloud-created group named COMPLIANCE_TEAM originally contained external guest users (Cloud-only members). When Azure AD Connect synchronization overwrote the cloud group with the synchronized on‑prem group (different Object ID), the external guest accounts that had been members of the cloud-native group were no longer present in the synchronized group membership that SharePoint used for access control, causing external guests to lose access.
[Data: Entities (540); Relationships (1007, 673)]

This shows a specific, high‑impact consequence from the Object‑ID overwrite pattern: loss of access for external collaborators. Because the cloud group and the synchronized on‑prem group were not identical in membership (cloud group had external guests; on‑prem did not), the synchronization process effectively removed those external members from the Active membership set used by SharePoint. The event underlines the need for pre‑sync membership comparisons and explicit handling of cloud‑only guest accounts when applying directory sync operations.
[Data: Relationships (1007); Entities (540)]

## System engineering used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate and compare group membership during analysis

Microsoft Graph was used as the API tool to inspect and review group membership; specifically, System Engineer 14 performed Graph queries to compare the Cloud and synchronized On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM groups and identify mismatched membership sets (for example, the presence of external guests in the Cloud group and internal users in the synchronized group). This indicates the investigation relied on authoritative directory API queries to validate membership differences and Object IDs.
[Data: Entities (544); Relationships (1015)]

The use of Graph queries supports reproducible diagnostics: membership enumeration via Graph can be scripted to produce audits of cloud‑only vs synchronized groups, to capture Object IDs and member lists, and to support reconciliation workflows. This established diagnostic practice is important operationally because it provides a reliable way to detect and verify membership divergences prior to remedial synchronization or ACL changes.
[Data: Entities (544); Relationships (1015)]

## Hybrid‑joined devices experienced delayed device synchronization causing stale compliance state and Conditional Access rejections until sync completed

Hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices used by affected users experienced delayed synchronization of device state into Entra ID; the delay was observed to be roughly 6–8 hours in this dataset. Because Conditional Access evaluates device compliance state recorded in Entra ID, devices with stale or "unknown" compliance status were evaluated as non‑compliant/unknown at the time of sign‑in, producing access or policy enforcement anomalies until the device sync completed and the compliance state was refreshed.
[Data: Entities (1355, 1357); Relationships (2731)]

Operationally, delayed device synchronization is a distinct failure mode from the group/Object‑ID overwrite issue, but it similarly affects effective access. The record indicates manual device synchronization was used as a remediation step to accelerate compliance status updates, and that at least one user (Employee 21) manually triggered a device sync to expedite Conditional Access re‑evaluation. This suggests both a tactical workaround available to users/operators and a need for improved sync reliability or monitoring to prevent repeated user impact.
[Data: Relationships (2712); Entities (1355, 1357)]

## Cloud sign‑in remained functional after UPN suffix changes, isolating the problem to authorization (group membership) rather than authentication

The Entra ID record notes that cloud sign‑in remained functional for the affected users after their cloud UPNs were updated to the new kf70group.local suffix during the second migration wave. This indicates that authentication and token issuance were successful following the migration change, and the observed service impact was primarily in authorization—driven by group membership evaluation and Object‑ID differences—rather than by a break in authentication flows.
[Data: Entities (366); Relationships (673)]

This differentiation is operationally important: because sign‑in/authentication worked, remediation focus should prioritize group/object reconciliation, Azure AD Connect filter/merge strategy, and SharePoint ACL verification (membership by Object ID) instead of troubleshooting token issuance or baseline sign‑in functionality. That separation narrows the scope of corrective actions and helps prioritize fixes that restore access for external guests and other affected members.
[Data: Entities (366); Relationships (673)]

## Recommended prioritized mitigations: reconcile Object IDs, establish pre‑sync validations, and improve device sync monitoring

Based on the documented chain of events, priority mitigations are: 1) reconcile and canonicalize group Object IDs and group nesting used by SharePoint across cloud and on‑prem sources before large‑scale syncs; 2) implement pre‑synchronization membership comparisons and explicit handling of cloud‑only guest accounts to avoid accidental removal of external collaborators; and 3) improve device synchronization monitoring/alerting and reduce device sync latency (or provide automated re‑sync) to prevent Conditional Access mis‑evaluations. Each of these actions addresses a directly observed failure mode in the data: the cloud group overwrite and guest exclusion (Object ID/membership mismatch) and the 6–8 hour device sync delay that left devices in an unknown compliance state.
[Data: Relationships (673, 1007, 2731); Entities (540, 1357)]

Operational steps that follow from these mitigations include: implementing an audit script (using Microsoft Graph) to compare object IDs and membership sets for groups that exist both as cloud objects and as synchronized on‑prem objects; creating a change‑control checklist for Azure AD Connect filter or suffix additions (including a rollback plan and pre‑sync membership report); and adding metrics/alerts for device sync latency and device compliance 'unknown' counts so platform owners can detect regressions quickly. The existence of Microsoft Graph usage in the investigation demonstrates that these scripted audits are feasible using existing tooling and can be incorporated into the change control and runbook process.
[Data: Entities (544); Relationships (1015, 673, 2731)]

REPORT 209:
# Entra ID, Azure AD Connect, COMPLIANCE_TEAM and Hybrid Device Sync — Identity & Access Community

This community centers on the customer's Entra ID (Azure AD) tenant as the authoritative identity and access control plane for cloud sign‑in, group evaluation, Conditional Access, and device compliance state. Azure AD Connect synchronization (adjusted during a second migration wave to include the kf70group.local suffix) provisioned on‑prem objects into Entra ID and replaced an existing cloud‑native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with a synchronized on‑prem group that had a different Object ID. That Object‑ID replacement, combined with group nesting/evaluation behavior in Entra ID and SharePoint Online, produced an access mismatch that excluded external guest accounts. Separately, hybrid Azure AD–joined devices experienced delayed device synchronization (6–8 hours), causing device compliance to be stale/unknown and Conditional Access to evaluate sessions as non‑compliant until manual or subsequent sync completed; at least one affected user (Employee 21) manually triggered a device sync to expedite re‑evaluation. System Engineers used Microsoft Graph to enumerate and compare group memberships to support the analysis. These interactions identify operational dependencies (Entra ID, Azure AD Connect, SharePoint Online, hybrid device sync), recurring failure modes (group/object‑ID overwrite and device sync latency), and clear stakeholder touchpoints (synchronization operators, system engineers, affected users). [Data: Entities (366, 540, 1355, 1357, 544); Relationships (673, 1007, 2731, 2712, 1015)]

## Entra ID is the authoritative identity plane and the locus of the access-evaluation behavior

Entra ID (Azure AD) is documented as the tenant that manages user and group identities, records device compliance state, issues authentication tokens used by cloud services (including SharePoint Online), and produces sign‑in logs with Conditional Access evaluation results. This places Entra ID at the center of both authentication (token issuance, cloud sign‑in) and authorization (group/object‑ID evaluation used by SharePoint Online) for the affected users.
[Data: Entities (366)]

Because Entra ID evaluates groups by Object ID and records device compliance status (with several devices shown as "unknown"), misalignment in group Object IDs or stale device compliance states directly impact effective permissions and Conditional Access decisions. The report explicitly notes that a misconfigured or incorrect group nesting, combined with Object‑ID based evaluation and duplicate/unequal groups across sources, was identified as the root cause of the access mismatch—establishing an operational dependency where Entra ID’s evaluation logic determines access outcomes in SharePoint Online and Conditional Access. This makes Entra ID the control point for corrective actions such as reconciling object IDs, fixing group nests, or remediating device compliance visibility.
[Data: Entities (366)]

## Azure AD Connect synchronization during the second migration wave overwrote a cloud-native group with a synchronized on‑prem object (Object ID change)

Azure AD Connect was updated during the second migration wave to include the kf70group.local suffix, which made on‑prem identities eligible for synchronization into Entra ID; as a result Azure AD Connect wrote synchronized on‑prem objects into Entra ID and caused a pre‑existing cloud‑native group with the same name to be overwritten by the synchronized on‑prem group. The overwrite produced a different Object ID in Entra ID for the group, creating a divergence between the previously referenced cloud group and the newly synchronized object.
[Data: Relationships (673); Entities (366, 540)]

That Object‑ID replacement is the core technical mechanism that caused access mismatches: services and ACLs that reference the original cloud group by Object ID (or depend on the previous membership) observed a different membership set after synchronization. Because Entra ID evaluates effective permissions by Object ID, replacing or swapping objects with identical names but different Object IDs leads to authorization changes even when display names appear unchanged—an important operational risk when enabling large‑scale synchronization or changing AD Connect filters. This traces the causal chain from a sync/filter change to an authorization impact in cloud services.
[Data: Relationships (673); Entities (366)]

## COMPLIANCE_TEAM cloud-native group originally contained external guests and those guests lost effective access when the group was overwritten

A cloud-created group named COMPLIANCE_TEAM originally contained external guest users (Cloud-only members). When Azure AD Connect synchronization overwrote the cloud group with the synchronized on‑prem group (different Object ID), the external guest accounts that had been members of the cloud-native group were no longer present in the synchronized group membership that SharePoint used for access control, causing external guests to lose access.
[Data: Entities (540); Relationships (1007, 673)]

This shows a specific, high‑impact consequence from the Object‑ID overwrite pattern: loss of access for external collaborators. Because the cloud group and the synchronized on‑prem group were not identical in membership (cloud group had external guests; on‑prem did not), the synchronization process effectively removed those external members from the Active membership set used by SharePoint. The event underlines the need for pre‑sync membership comparisons and explicit handling of cloud‑only guest accounts when applying directory sync operations.
[Data: Relationships (1007); Entities (540)]

## System engineering used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate and compare group membership during analysis

Microsoft Graph was used as the API tool to inspect and review group membership; specifically, System Engineer 14 performed Graph queries to compare the Cloud and synchronized On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM groups and identify mismatched membership sets (for example, the presence of external guests in the Cloud group and internal users in the synchronized group). This indicates the investigation relied on authoritative directory API queries to validate membership differences and Object IDs.
[Data: Entities (544); Relationships (1015)]

The use of Graph queries supports reproducible diagnostics: membership enumeration via Graph can be scripted to produce audits of cloud‑only vs synchronized groups, to capture Object IDs and member lists, and to support reconciliation workflows. This established diagnostic practice is important operationally because it provides a reliable way to detect and verify membership divergences prior to remedial synchronization or ACL changes.
[Data: Entities (544); Relationships (1015)]

## Hybrid‑joined devices experienced delayed device synchronization causing stale compliance state and Conditional Access rejections until sync completed

Hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices used by affected users experienced delayed synchronization of device state into Entra ID; the delay was observed to be roughly 6–8 hours in this dataset. Because Conditional Access evaluates device compliance state recorded in Entra ID, devices with stale or "unknown" compliance status were evaluated as non‑compliant/unknown at the time of sign‑in, producing access or policy enforcement anomalies until the device sync completed and the compliance state was refreshed.
[Data: Entities (1355, 1357); Relationships (2731)]

Operationally, delayed device synchronization is a distinct failure mode from the group/Object‑ID overwrite issue, but it similarly affects effective access. The record indicates manual device synchronization was used as a remediation step to accelerate compliance status updates, and that at least one user (Employee 21) manually triggered a device sync to expedite Conditional Access re‑evaluation. This suggests both a tactical workaround available to users/operators and a need for improved sync reliability or monitoring to prevent repeated user impact.
[Data: Relationships (2712); Entities (1355, 1357)]

## Cloud sign‑in remained functional after UPN suffix changes, isolating the problem to authorization (group membership) rather than authentication

The Entra ID record notes that cloud sign‑in remained functional for the affected users after their cloud UPNs were updated to the new kf70group.local suffix during the second migration wave. This indicates that authentication and token issuance were successful following the migration change, and the observed service impact was primarily in authorization—driven by group membership evaluation and Object‑ID differences—rather than by a break in authentication flows.
[Data: Entities (366); Relationships (673)]

This differentiation is operationally important: because sign‑in/authentication worked, remediation focus should prioritize group/object reconciliation, Azure AD Connect filter/merge strategy, and SharePoint ACL verification (membership by Object ID) instead of troubleshooting token issuance or baseline sign‑in functionality. That separation narrows the scope of corrective actions and helps prioritize fixes that restore access for external guests and other affected members.
[Data: Entities (366); Relationships (673)]

## Recommended prioritized mitigations: reconcile Object IDs, establish pre‑sync validations, and improve device sync monitoring

Based on the documented chain of events, priority mitigations are: 1) reconcile and canonicalize group Object IDs and group nesting used by SharePoint across cloud and on‑prem sources before large‑scale syncs; 2) implement pre‑synchronization membership comparisons and explicit handling of cloud‑only guest accounts to avoid accidental removal of external collaborators; and 3) improve device synchronization monitoring/alerting and reduce device sync latency (or provide automated re‑sync) to prevent Conditional Access mis‑evaluations. Each of these actions addresses a directly observed failure mode in the data: the cloud group overwrite and guest exclusion (Object ID/membership mismatch) and the 6–8 hour device sync delay that left devices in an unknown compliance state.
[Data: Relationships (673, 1007, 2731); Entities (540, 1357)]

Operational steps that follow from these mitigations include: implementing an audit script (using Microsoft Graph) to compare object IDs and membership sets for groups that exist both as cloud objects and as synchronized on‑prem objects; creating a change‑control checklist for Azure AD Connect filter or suffix additions (including a rollback plan and pre‑sync membership report); and adding metrics/alerts for device sync latency and device compliance 'unknown' counts so platform owners can detect regressions quickly. The existence of Microsoft Graph usage in the investigation demonstrates that these scripted audits are feasible using existing tooling and can be incorporated into the change control and runbook process.
[Data: Entities (544); Relationships (1015, 673, 2731)]

SOURCE 63:
 be explicitly reviewed before each migration wave
- UPN updates should be applied cloud-only (not bidirectional) unless explicitly required
- Service accounts should be excluded from baseline Conditional Access policies via dedicated exclusion groups
- Group structure changes must verify that policy assignments (CA, sensitivity labels, app deployments, license assignments) reference both old and new groups during transition
- Each migration wave should have an explicit rollback runbook
- After each wave, a validation pass should test: cloud sign-in, on-prem app sign-in, mobile email, scheduled tasks for service accounts, group membership cross-environment

Common cascade patterns
- UPN suffix change cascade: one configuration change in UPN suffix policy can affect cloud sign-in, on-prem app authentication, and any service that matches users by UPN
- AADC scope change cascade: adding or removing OUs from sync scope affects all dependent group memberships, license assignments, and policy applicability
- Conditional Access enforcement cascade: enabling a tenant baseline CA policy affects interactive sign-ins (intended) and may unintentionally affect non-interactive service-account logons
- Modern Auth enforcement cascade: tenant-level modern-auth requirement breaks legacy mail clients on migrated users that previously used on-prem Basic Auth

References
- Microsoft Entra Connect documentation (UPN handling, attribute flow)
- Conditional Access best practices (service account exclusion patterns)
- Modern Authentication migration guide (mobile device handling)
- Internal migration runbook KF-MIG-RB-v1.4 (wave validation checklist)

31.01.25 16:34 Ticket closed | TI-1700004
Closure description:
Root cause was twofold. First, the service accounts were placed in the "KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid" group, which is the group targeted by the new tenant-baseline Conditional Access policy "KF70-Require-MFA". The Conditional Access evaluation was therefore being applied to non-interactive logons for service accounts, breaking scheduled-task authentication where MFA cannot be satisfied. Second, the service accounts had been moved to the new OU which is filtered IN by Azure AD Connect, leading to the accounts being synchronized to Entra ID and then having a license assigned (small but non-zero impact on the on-prem authentication path because of mfaPreferred attribute conflicts).
Resolution: Created a sub-group "KF70-ServiceAccounts-NoMFA" containing the service accounts and added it to the Conditional Access policy as an explicit exclusion. Re-evaluated AAD Connect filter to keep the on-prem service-account authentication path clean. Validated that scheduled tasks are now running successfully overnight.
Documentation note: For future migrations, service accounts must be explicitly excluded from baseline CA policies via a dedicated exclusion group, and their AAD Connect sync status should be reviewed to avoid unintended cloud-side license assignment. We have added this to the migration runbook.

Operationally, delayed device synchronization is a distinct failure mode from the group/Object‑ID overwrite issue, but it similarly affects effective access. The record indicates manual device synchronization was used as a remediation step to accelerate compliance status updates, and that at least one user (Employee 21) manually triggered a device sync to expedite Conditional Access re‑evaluation. This suggests both a tactical workaround available to users/operators and a need for improved sync reliability or monitoring to prevent repeated user impact.
[Data: Relationships (2712); Entities (1355, 1357)]

## Cloud sign‑in remained functional after UPN suffix changes, isolating the problem to authorization (group membership) rather than authentication

The Entra ID record notes that cloud sign‑in remained functional for the affected users after their cloud UPNs were updated to the new kf70group.local suffix during the second migration wave. This indicates that authentication and token issuance were successful following the migration change, and the observed service impact was primarily in authorization—driven by group membership evaluation and Object‑ID differences—rather than by a break in authentication flows.
[Data: Entities (366); Relationships (673)]

This differentiation is operationally important: because sign‑in/authentication worked, remediation focus should prioritize group/object reconciliation, Azure AD Connect filter/merge strategy, and SharePoint ACL verification (membership by Object ID) instead of troubleshooting token issuance or baseline sign‑in functionality. That separation narrows the scope of corrective actions and helps prioritize fixes that restore access for external guests and other affected members.
[Data: Entities (366); Relationships (673)]

## Recommended prioritized mitigations: reconcile Object IDs, establish pre‑sync validations, and improve device sync monitoring

Based on the documented chain of events, priority mitigations are: 1) reconcile and canonicalize group Object IDs and group nesting used by SharePoint across cloud and on‑prem sources before large‑scale syncs; 2) implement pre‑synchronization membership comparisons and explicit handling of cloud‑only guest accounts to avoid accidental removal of external collaborators; and 3) improve device synchronization monitoring/alerting and reduce device sync latency (or provide automated re‑sync) to prevent Conditional Access mis‑evaluations. Each of these actions addresses a directly observed failure mode in the data: the cloud group overwrite and guest exclusion (Object ID/membership mismatch) and the 6–8 hour device sync delay that left devices in an unknown compliance state.
[Data: Relationships (673, 1007, 2731); Entities (540, 1357)]

Operational steps that follow from these mitigations include: implementing an audit script (using Microsoft Graph) to compare object IDs and membership sets for groups that exist both as cloud objects and as synchronized on‑prem objects; creating a change‑control checklist for Azure AD Connect filter or suffix additions (including a rollback plan and pre‑sync membership report); and adding metrics/alerts for device sync latency and device compliance 'unknown' counts so platform owners can detect regressions quickly. The existence of Microsoft Graph usage in the investigation demonstrates that these scripted audits are feasible using existing tooling and can be incorporated into the change control and runbook process.
[Data: Entities (544); Relationships (1015, 673, 2731)]

 you mention Conditional Access: as part of the migration baseline a CA policy was enabled requiring MFA for the "KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid" group. Could that be the issue?

31.01.25 16:34 Ticket closed | TI-1700004
Closure description:
Root cause was twofold. First, the service accounts were placed in the "KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid" group, which is the group targeted by the new tenant-baseline Conditional Access policy "KF70-Require-MFA". The Conditional Access evaluation was therefore being applied to non-interactive logons for service accounts, breaking scheduled-task authentication where MFA cannot be satisfied. Second, the service accounts had been moved to the new OU which is filtered IN by Azure AD Connect, leading to the accounts being synchronized to Entra ID and then having a license assigned (small but non-zero impact on the on-prem authentication path because of mfaPreferred attribute conflicts).
Resolution: Created a sub-group "KF70-ServiceAccounts-NoMFA" containing the service accounts and added it to the Conditional Access policy as an explicit exclusion. Re-evaluated AAD Connect filter to keep the on-prem service-account authentication path clean. Validated that scheduled tasks are now running successfully overnight.
Documentation note: For future migrations, service accounts must be explicitly excluded from baseline CA policies via a dedicated exclusion group, and their AAD Connect sync status should be reviewed to avoid unintended cloud-side license assignment. We have added this to the migration runbook.

First, the service accounts were placed in the "KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid" group, which is the group targeted by the new tenant-baseline Conditional Access policy "KF70-Require-MFA". The Conditional Access evaluation was therefore being applied to non-interactive logons for service accounts, breaking scheduled-task authentication where MFA cannot be satisfied. Second, the service accounts had been moved to the new OU which is filtered IN by Azure AD Connect, leading to the accounts being synchronized to Entra ID and then having a license assigned (small but non-zero impact on the on-prem authentication path because of mfaPreferred attribute conflicts).
Resolution: Created a sub-group "KF70-ServiceAccounts-NoMFA" containing the service accounts and added it to the Conditional Access policy as an explicit exclusion. Re-evaluated AAD Connect filter to keep the on-prem service-account authentication path clean. Validated that scheduled tasks are now running successfully overnight.
Documentation note: For future migrations, service accounts must be explicitly excluded from baseline CA policies via a dedicated exclusion group, and their AAD Connect sync status should be reviewed to avoid unintended cloud-side license assignment. We have added this to the migration runbook.

=== CITED SOURCES ===
SOURCE 63:
 be explicitly reviewed before each migration wave
- UPN updates should be applied cloud-only (not bidirectional) unless explicitly required
- Service accounts should be excluded from baseline Conditional Access policies via dedicated exclusion groups
- Group structure changes must verify that policy assignments (CA, sensitivity labels, app deployments, license assignments) reference both old and new groups during transition
- Each migration wave should have an explicit rollback runbook
- After each wave, a validation pass should test: cloud sign-in, on-prem app sign-in, mobile email, scheduled tasks for service accounts, group membership cross-environment

Common cascade patterns
- UPN suffix change cascade: one configuration change in UPN suffix policy can affect cloud sign-in, on-prem app authentication, and any service that matches users by UPN
- AADC scope change cascade: adding or removing OUs from sync scope affects all dependent group memberships, license assignments, and policy applicability
- Conditional Access enforcement cascade: enabling a tenant baseline CA policy affects interactive sign-ins (intended) and may unintentionally affect non-interactive service-account logons
- Modern Auth enforcement cascade: tenant-level modern-auth requirement breaks legacy mail clients on migrated users that previously used on-prem Basic Auth

References
- Microsoft Entra Connect documentation (UPN handling, attribute flow)
- Conditional Access best practices (service account exclusion patterns)
- Modern Authentication migration guide (mobile device handling)
- Internal migration runbook KF-MIG-RB-v1.4 (wave validation checklist)

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: The four different‑looking symptoms in your ticket map to a known single class of hybrid‑identity migration failures documented in the indexed records: (a) provisioning/template UPN writeback, (b) AAD Connect OU/sync scope errors causing group/object divergence, (c) legacy Basic‑Auth mobile clients blocked by Modern Auth enforcement, and (d) service accounts becoming subject to interactive MFA via CA after being synchronized. All of those fixes are recorded in the incidents cited above
